feedback.text: Good evening, I am an Italian citizen that was informed about the opportunity to give my feedback on this matter, after also participating on another European Citizen Initiative that I think would (and could) be potentially related to this one. As far as consumer protection goes, I'm perpetually convinced there are many blind spots in existing legislation that give too much leeway to big and small players in the digital industry; leeway that in the end is used and abused (either voluntarily or for a lack of care towards the very customers that keep the industry afloat), to maximize revenue in a never ending chase to the next profitable quarter. It's always been a race between those who try to limit this often self destructing chase, and those who, by trial and error, find breaches in the wall of norms and rules. I'm not exactly a fan of legislation, since the intricacies of the subject often evade my understanding, and I'm sure it is the same for an abundant amount of the "civilian" population, but it's clear that trusting any industry, in which the main purpose is growth in revenue, to self regulate on the basis of what is ethical or just and not on what is profitable borders on ludicrous. Not for the lack of individual players that would respect the dignity of the ones they provide service to, but because the former would inevitably be outcompeted by the entities that don't, thus necessitating a great equalizer that is external to this playing field and has no stake (monetary, at least) in the matter. Having said that, knowing the work done in all of these years by people far more competent than me, seeing with my own eyes the spearheading of legislation aimed at protecting European customers (and by extension even those who couldn't have a say in the matter, but that I'm sure benefit nonetheless, all around the world), I'm elated to read in the attached document that I'm not the only one that thinks there is still work to be done. The lack of transparency regarding ownership of digital content, be it movies or video games, just to cite the few that in particular I see myself consuming and appreciating on a regular basis, hurts. Undermines trust. Robs us of memories and (alleged) possessions, duly paid for, under guarantees and agreements that end up being trampled on in a not so distant future. Evident example of this could be the infamous erasure of The Crew from libraries of paying customers, or the countable but ever more numerous instances of shrinking or outright deletion of content that was paid, and "owned", but not in any meaningful way. The predatory solutions so often used under the guise of legitimate game mechanics, and sold as such, end up exposing not only minors but also vulnerable people to a danger carefully crafted to make you act on impulse, chasing a high that in extreme cases robs you of funds to live a respectable life, not unlike those who suffer from gambling addiction. The dark pattern I personally had the displeasure of hearing about, regarding a game called Diablo 4, still makes me very hesitant to buy any product in which I hear there are aggressive monetization practices, especially if the game was already paid for. Too many instances of "solutions" to increase revenue that ended up being the crutches upon which major actors in the digital industry propped themselves and based their entire development, causing a steady but inevitable lack of quality in the end product. There is a time where even they have to be saved from themselves. Actual measures that render ownership of digital content reality, the regulation of the predatory mechanics that plague this space are meant to encounter extreme opposition from those that benefit from them, it goes without saying, even if it means collapse of the industry I can only hope that this destructive greed can be kept in check. I thank you in advance for reading this message, hoping that it will make even the slightest bit of difference.
